title: 8 Managing Transaction Alerts
body_text:
8	Managing Transaction Alerts
8.1	Overview of Managing Transaction Alerts
When Enterprise Fraud Management (EFM) generates an alert within an incident, your fraud team receives an email notification of the alert, including the related transaction ID. See Figure 1. The alerted transaction within that incident is put on hold until you investigate the transaction and send the result of your investigation to Central 1.
Important:
To manage who receives EFM email notifications at your financial institution, contact Client Support Services at
support@central1.com
.
Figure 1: Example of Email Notification for Alerted Transactions
YouГўВЂВ™ll receive additional email notifications if any subsequent transactions are initiated within that same incident before you complete an investigation on the initial transaction. See Figure 2. EFM flags any subsequent transactions with an alert triggered by the rule ГўВЂВњincidentUnderReviewГўВЂВќ. EFM automatically rejects subsequent Interac
Г‚В®
e-Transfer transactions and flags them as Auto Risk.
Figure 2: Example of Email Notification for Auto Risk Interac e-Transfer Transactions
Auto Risk flagged transactions must be investigated and the following actions must be performed:
If your investigation determines that an Interac e-Transfer transaction flagged as Auto Risk is legitimate, identify the transaction as No-risk. Contact your customer and ask them to resend the transaction. If the alerted transaction is identified as fraudulent but the Auto Risk flagged transaction is legitimate, both transactions must be actioned accordingly. See Section 8.4, Identifying Alerts in Auto Risk Transactions, for more information. If your investigation determines that the transaction flagged as Auto Risk is fraudulent, you need to add a manual alert and identify it as a Risk in EFM. This transaction doesnГўВЂВ™t need to be cancelled, as Auto Risk flags stop transactions before they reach Interac.
YouГўВЂВ™ll also receive email notifications when manual alerts are created on transactions by Central 1ГўВЂВ™s FAS team. The email notifications include the transaction alert ID. See Figure 3.
Figure 3: Example of Email Notification When a Manual Alert is Created
The Activity Timeline in an incident displays events that identify whether a transaction was rejected or successfully transmitted. See Figure 4. A rejected Interac
e-Transfer transaction that was triggered by the rules will only show the paymentRT event. Successfully transmitted Interac
e-transfer transactions show the paymentRT and paymentNRT events (both displaying the same transaction ID and amount). As wire transactions are processed differently than Interac e-Transfer transactions, they will display only the wireRT event. PaymentRT is the event for the initiated Interac e-Transfer that indicates the real time when the transaction was initiated.
paymentNRT is the event that indicates the payment went through to Interac.
wireRT is the event when a customer initiates a wire payment.
Figure 4: Example of Activity Timeline
The following actions are required when managing alerts in EFM:
Note:
Training videos
are available for the procedures in this chapter.
Review transactions within an incident that are marked with an Alert and identify them as Risk or No-risk. See Section 8.2, Identifying a Transaction as Fraudulent, or Section 8.3, Identifying a Transaction as Legitimate.
You can identify alerted transactions in bulk if an incident contains multiple alerts that have the same classification. You can also identify each alerted transaction within the incident separately if the classifications vary. See Section 8.4, Identifying Alerts in Auto Risk Transactions.
Change the classification of a previously identified alert if new information indicates that a previous identification no longer applies (e.g., a transaction alert identified as legitimate is discovered to be fraudulent). See Section 8.7, Revising a Previously Identified Alert.
Create manual alerts for unalerted transactions and identify them as fraudulent (e.g., a customer reports fraud on a transaction that wasnГўВЂВ™t flagged in EFM). See Section 8.5, Adding Manual Alerts to Unalerted Transactions, and Section 8.11, Using the Search Function.
Create manual alerts for transactions flagged as Auto Risk if the transaction is fraudulent. See Section 8.4, Identifying Alerts in Auto Risk Transactions.
When you identify a transaction as a Risk or No-risk, you must provide a reason. We highly recommend also adding a comment. Comments provide an explanation for the classification of an alert and are displayed in the Activity Timeline for easy reference. If no comment is provided, youГўВЂВ™ll only see the classification (i.e., Risk or No-risk) in the Activity Timeline. Ensure comments are appropriate for EFM users and customers, clear, succinct, and descriptive. A comment cannot exceed 400 characters. Add an additional comment if needed.
Important
:
Comments are visible on other downstream systems such as the Interac eГўВЂВ‘Transfer Online Administration System (OAS) or other banking applications. Comments must be appropriate for both user and customer audiences.
8.2	Identifying a Transaction as Fraudulent
When an investigation indicates that a transaction is fraudulent, youГўВЂВ™ll identify the alerted transaction as Risk in EFM.
To identify an alerted transaction as fraudulent:
Log in to EFM as described in Section 5.2, Logging in to Enterprise Fraud Management, then select
Incidents
on the Dashboard page.
Select the
Customer ID
to open the Incident Review page. See Figure 5.
Figure 5: Selecting the Customer ID
Complete the information in the sidebar. See Figures 6 and 7.
ГўВ‘
Risk
ГўВЂВ“ Select
Risk
in the sidebar.
Figure 6: Selecting ГўВЂВњRiskГўВЂВќ
ГўВ‘ВЎ
Risk Severity
ГўВЂВ“ Defaults to Confirmed. Change to Suspected, if applicable. See Figure 7.
ГўВ‘Вў
Risk Reasons
ГўВЂВ“ Select a risk reason from the drop-down menu.
Important
:	Selecting the appropriate risk severity and risk reason is critical. The risk severity of a transaction indicates if itГўВЂВ™s been confirmed fraudulent by the customer or only suspected of fraudulent activity and the confirmation of fraudulent activity hasnГўВЂВ™t been obtained yet. Some risk reasons are based on Interac
ГўВЂВ™s
standardized and approved list and are part of the reporting obligation for Interac e-Transfer services. Selecting the applicable reason is also important for Central 1ГўВЂВ™s ongoing reporting and monitoring.
You can select one or more reasons as to why a transaction is deemed fraudulent. Several of the reasons begin with SCAM, and four of those contain the text ANR (see table below). These four specific reasons are only to be used when the payment transaction is a real-time payment and has been sent using Account Number Routing (ANR). The table outlines the definition of each reason to guide you in selecting the appropriate reason.
Risk Reason
Applicable Use
Account Takeover
Fraudsters gained access to an account using stolen or compromised credentials and were able to perform transactions posing as the legitimate account holder.
Application Fraud
Fraudsters used either synthetic IDs, falsified information, or stolen personal information to open bank accounts or ГўВЂВњfalse applicationsГўВЂВќ to receive funds.
Buyer/Seller Scam
Customer authorized the sending of funds for goods, services, or rental purposes; however, the ad was placed solely to defraud unsuspecting victims for financial gain.
Intercepted Transfer
Customer authorized the sending of the funds; however, the transfer was maliciously deposited by someone other than the intended recipient.
Cheque ГўВЂВ“ Altered
Fraudsters alter the details on a cheque, such as changing the payeeГўВЂВ™s name, the amount, or the date.
Cheque ГўВЂВ“ Counterfeit
Counterfeit cheques are fake cheques created to look like legitimate ones, often using copied cheque stock.
Cheque ГўВЂВ“ Duplicate Item
Fraudsters create duplicate cheques to be presented multiple times, to withdraw more money than is available in the account.
Cheque ГўВЂВ“ Forged Endorsement / Intended Payee Not Paid
Forged Endorsement / Intended Payee Not Paid: Fraudsters deposit cheques belonging to someone else.
Cheque ГўВЂВ“ Kiting
Cheque kiting is a form of fraud where someone writes a cheque from an account that does not have sufficient funds, but they deposit it into another account with the hope that the bank will clear the cheque before it bounces.
Investment/Loan Scam
Customer authorized the sending of funds as part of a return on investment or deposit for a loan; however, the advertisement was placed solely to defraud unsuspecting victims for financial gain.
Job Scam
Customer authorized the sending of funds after falling victim to a false job advertisement placed solely to defraud unsuspecting victims for financial gain.
Romance Scam
Customer authorized the sending of the funds to a criminal who used the illusion of a romantic or close relationship for financial gain.
SCAM ГўВЂВ“ Business Email Compromise ГўВЂВ“ eГўВЂВ‘Transfer for Business (ANR) ONLY
Fraudsters impersonated a trusted person, either a senior executive, trusted vendor, or company employee, using company information to convince an employee to generate a payment.
Only use for real-time, account-number-routed (ANR) payments.
SCAM ГўВЂВ“ Other
Customer authorized the sending of the funds for a false product, service, or opportunity designed solely to defraud unsuspecting victims for financial gain.
SCAM ГўВЂВ“ Vendor Email Compromise ГўВЂВ“ eГўВЂВ‘Transfer for Business (ANR) ONLY
Fraudsters impersonated a trusted person, either a senior executive, trusted vendor, or company employee, using company information to convince an employee to generate a payment.
Only use for real-time, account-number-routed (ANR) payments.
SCAM ГўВЂВ“ Fraudulent Business ГўВЂВ“ eГўВЂВ‘Transfer for Business (ANR) ONLY
A business set up for the sole purpose of performing fraudulent transactions. This can include selling false items or illegal goods or processing fraudulent payments, e.g., stolen/compromised cards.
Only use for real-time, account-number-routed (ANR) payments.
Thread Emergency Scam
Customer authorized the sending of funds to a criminal impersonating another individual or public organization (e.g., police officer, CRA, financial institution).
Important
:
The Initiate Fund Recovery box is strictly intended for confirmed Risk and completed Interac e-Transfer transactions created less than 90 days ago. Typically, confirmed Risk and completed transactions have had a manual alert added to them. See Section 8.5, Adding Manual Alerts to Unalerted Transactions, for more information. Do not use this box for mobile deposit transactions or wire transactions. After identifying the completed Interac e-Transfer transaction as Risk, an automated email with the transactionГўВЂВ™s details is sent to Central 1ГўВЂВ™s FAS team. EFM also sends existing EFM contacts a copy of the automated email. The FAS Team then begins the recovery of funds process. See Section 8.12, Recovery of Funds, for more information about the recovery of funds process.
Important
:	EFM users with OAS access who are assigned Fraud Analyst roles must initiate funds recovery requests through OAS instead of using EFMГўВЂВ™s Initiate Fund Recovery box. See the Interac eГўВЂВ‘Transfer Online Administration System Part for more information.
ГўВ‘ВЈ
Comment
(strongly recommended) ГўВЂВ“ Enter a comment or select one from the template drop-down menu. If you selected
SCAM ГўВЂВ“ Other
as the risk reason, you must provide a comment to clarify the type of scam. Ensure comments are appropriate for EFM users and customers, clear, succinct, and descriptive. Comments are displayed in the Activity Timeline. A comment canГўВЂВ™t exceed 400 characters. Add an additional comment if needed.
Important
:	Comments are visible on other downstream systems such as the Interac eГўВЂВ‘Transfer Online Administration System (OAS) or other banking applications. Comments must be appropriate for both user and customer audiences.
ГўВ‘В¤
Select
Complete review
.
Figure 7: Identifying Transaction as Fraudulent
The incident is moved to the Closed list.
To view the Closed list:
Select the Closed list as described in Section 7.2, Accessing the Incidents Page.
View the Closed list. All incidents are displayed with zero alerts, indicating that alerts have been classified and reported. See Figure 8.
Figure 8: Incident Moved to Closed List
Select an incident in the Closed list to see the actions in the Activity Timeline. See Figure 9.
Figure 9: Viewing Actions in Activity Timeline
Important
:	When an Interac e-Transfer transaction has an alert or an Auto Risk flag, your customer is unable to send transactions. Identifying the initial alerted transaction as Risk or No-risk and any fraudulent Auto Risk flagged transactions as Risk allows your customer to send transactions again. Any debited funds are returned to your customer. See Section 8.12, Recovery of Funds, for more information.
Related Information and Payment Solution Manual Parts
Interac eГўВЂВ‘Transfer Online Administration System (OAS) Part
Training Videos:
Fraud Alert Services in EFM
8.3	Identifying a Transaction as Legitimate
When an investigation indicates that a suspicious transaction is legitimate, youГўВЂВ™ll identify the alerted transaction as No-risk in EFM.
To identify an alerted transaction as legitimate:
Log in to EFM as described in Section 5.2, Logging in to Enterprise Fraud Management, then select
Incidents
on the Dashboard page.
Select the
Customer ID
to open the Incident Review page. See Figure 10.
Figure 10: Selecting the Customer ID
Complete the information in the sidebar. See Figures 11 and 12.
ГўВ‘
No-risk
ГўВЂВ“ Select
No-risk
.
Figure 11: Selecting No-risk
ГўВ‘ВЎ
No-risk reasons
ГўВЂВ“ Select a No-risk reason from the drop-down menu. See Figure 12.
You can select one reason as to why a transaction is legitimate. By selecting a no-risk reason, the alerted transaction is released and then completed. The table outlines the definition of each reason to guide you in selecting the appropriate reason.
No-risk Reason
Applicable Use
Confirmed Legitimate
Customer confirmed they authorized this transaction.
e-Transfer: Confirmed Legitimate ГўВЂВ“ Customer Wants to Cancel
Customer confirmed they authorized this transaction and they have requested for the transaction to be cancelled.
This no-risk reason is strictly intended for Interac e-Transfer transactions. Do not use this no-risk reason for a mobile deposit or a wire.
If this no-risk reason is selected for a wire, the wire wonГўВЂВ™t be cancelled.
Presumed Legitimate
Based on the transactionГўВЂВ™s investigation, itГўВЂВ™s likely the customer authorized the transaction. The customer hasnГўВЂВ™t explicitly confirmed they authorized the transaction yet.
Important
:	If you use the no-risk reason ГўВЂВњe-Transfer: Confirmed Legitimate ГўВЂВ“ Customer Wants to CancelГўВЂВќ, check OAS and the customerГўВЂВ™s account to ensure the transaction was cancelled and that the funds were returned to the customer.
ГўВ‘Вў
Comment
(strongly recommended) ГўВЂВ“ Enter a comment or select one from the template drop-down menu. Ensure comments are appropriate for EFM users and customers. Comments are displayed in the Activity Timeline. A comment canГўВЂВ™t exceed 400 characters. Add an additional comment if needed.
Important
: Comments are visible on other downstream systems such as the Interac eГўВЂВ‘Transfer Online Administration System (OAS) or other banking applications. Comments must be appropriate for both user and customer audiences.
ГўВ‘ВЈ
Select
Complete review
.
Figure 12: Identifying Transaction as Legitimate
The incident is moved to the Closed list.
To view the Closed list:
Select the Closed list as described in Section 7.2, Accessing the Incidents Page.
View the Closed list. All incidents in the Closed list are displayed with zero alerts, indicating that alerts have been classified and reported. See Figure 13.
Figure 13: Incident Moved to Closed List
Select an incident in the Closed list to see the actions in the Activity Timeline. See Figure 14.
Figure 14: Viewing Actions in Activity Timeline
Important
:	Since Auto Risk transactions are automatically rejected, advise your customer to re-initiate the transaction if they want to proceed with the payment if the transaction is legitimate.
Related Information
Training Videos:
Fraud Alert Services in EFM
8.4	Identifying Alerts in Auto Risk Transactions
When an incident has a pending alert, any subsequent transaction on that account will be automatically rejected with an Auto Risk flag. This provides our customers with better protection from loss. This situation also creates an incident with multiple alerted transactions that you can identify, as applicable, in bulk or individually.
Important
:	Auto Risk flags are only applicable to Interac e-Transfer transactions.
Note:	Rejecting a payment vs. putting subsequent payments on hold ensures that funds are not depleted and available for other transactions (i.e., ATM withdrawals).
To investigate an incident:
Log in to EFM as described in Section 5.2, Logging in to Enterprise Fraud Management, then select
Incidents
on the Dashboard page.
Select the Customer ID to open the Incident review page. See Figure 15.
Figure 15: Opening the Incident
The Activity Timeline indicates what type of alerts are in the incident. This example shows one transaction with an alert triggered by the rules in EFM. The second transaction is flagged with Auto Risk and automatically rejected because of the pending alert. See Figure 16.
Figure 16: Incident with a Pending Transaction and an Auto Risk Transaction
If you identify both transactions as fraudulent, youГўВЂВ™ll classify the incident as Risk. If you identify both transactions as legitimate, youГўВЂВ™ll classify the incident as No-risk.
To classify both transactions as Risk, you must add a manual alert to the Auto Risk transaction first. The process is the same if you need to identify both transactions as legitimate.
ГўВ‘
Select the eye icon in the Auto Risk transaction to open the sidebar. See Figure 17.
ГўВ‘ВЎ
Select
Create Alert
.
ГўВ‘Вў
Comment
(strongly recommended). Enter a comment or select one from the template drop-down menu. Ensure comments are appropriate for EFM users and customers, clear, succinct, and descriptive. A comment cannot exceed 400 characters. Add an additional comment if needed.
Important
:
Comments are visible on other downstream systems such as the Interac eГўВЂВ‘Transfer Online Administration System (OAS) or other banking applications. Comments must be appropriate for both user and customer audiences.
ГўВ‘ВЈ
Select
Add alert
.
Figure 17: Adding a Manual Alert to an Auto Risk Transaction
The Auto Risk flag changes to an Alert when you add a manual alert. See Figure 18.
Figure 18: Auto Risk Flag Replaced by Alert
Select
Risk
in the sidebar on the left and leave the check mark in the box beside ГўВЂВњApply to all risk alertsГўВЂВќ. In the Activity Timeline, youГўВЂВ™ll see all selected transactions classified as risk. See FigureВ 19.
Select a risk reason and enter a comment or select one from the template drop-down, then select
Complete Review
.
Important
:
Comments are visible on other downstream systems such as the Interac eГўВЂВ‘Transfer Online Administration System (OAS) or other banking applications. Comments must be appropriate for both user and customer audiences.
Figure 19: Classifying Transactions as Risk
Important
:	In some incidents, you might find that an alerted transaction is legitimate, but the Auto Risk transaction is fraudulent (e.g., CounterParty ID is different). Alternatively, it may be that an alerted transaction is fraudulent, but the Auto Risk transaction is legitimate. In either case, identify each transaction individually.
When identifying individual transactions with different classifications, you must first add a manual alert to the Auto Risk transaction.
ГўВ‘
Select the eye icon in the Auto Risk transaction to open the sidebar. See Figure 20.
ГўВ‘ВЎ
Select
Create Alert
.
ГўВ‘Вў
Comment
(strongly recommended). Enter a comment or select one from the template drop-down menu. Ensure comments are appropriate for EFM users and customers, clear, succinct, and descriptive. A comment cannot exceed 400 characters. Add an additional comment if needed.
Important
:
Comments are visible on other downstream systems such as the Interac eГўВЂВ‘Transfer Online Administration System (OAS) or other banking applications. Comments must be appropriate for both user and customer audiences.
ГўВ‘ВЈ
Select
Add alert
.
Figure 20: Adding a Manual Alert to an Auto Risk Transaction
This changes the Auto Risk flag to a ГўВЂВњManually created alertГўВЂВќ. See Figure 21.
Figure 21: Auto Risk Flag Replaced by Alert
To mark transactions individually:
ГўВ‘
Select
Risk
or
No-risk
in the sidebar to open the Activity Timeline. Whichever you choose first will apply to both transactions. See Figure 22.
ГўВ‘ВЎ
Remove the check marks beside ГўВЂВњApply to all Risk alertsГўВЂВќ and ГўВЂВњApply to all No-risk alertsГўВЂВќ.
ГўВ‘Вў
Select
Risk
in the Activity Timeline for the transaction with the manually created alert, then select the pencil icon to open the editing fields.
ГўВ‘ВЈ
Select a Risk reason.
ГўВ‘В¤
Select
No-risk
in the Activity Timeline for the transaction alerted by EFM, then select the pencil icon to open the editing fields.
ГўВ‘ВҐ
Select a No-risk reason.
ГўВ‘В¦
Select
Complete review
.
Figure 22: Identifying Alerted Transactions Individually
The incident is moved to the Closed list. Incidents in the Closed list are displayed with zero alerts, indicating that alerts have been classified. See Figure 23.
Figure 23: Incident Moved to Closed List
You can select an incident in the Closed list to see the actions in the Activity Timeline. See FigureВ 24.
Figure 24: Viewing Actions in Activity Timeline
Note:	When a legitimate transaction is rejected with an Auto Risk flag, let your customer know that they must re-send the transaction if they wish to continue with it.
Related Information
Training Videos:
Fraud Alert Services in EFM
8.5	Adding Manual Alerts to Unalerted Transactions
A manual alert is an alert that an EFM user adds to a transaction that wasnГўВЂВ™t initially alerted by EFM. A manual alert may also be added by Central 1ГўВЂВ™s Fraud Solutions team if a transaction passes through EFM but is identified as suspicious upon further investigation.
When a transaction is alerted in EFM, youГўВЂВ™ll receive an email notification. The notification occurs when EFM creates alerts and when an alert is added by Central 1. If you receive a notification of an alert, you must investigate the alert to determine if the transaction is fraudulent or not. See Section 8.2, Identifying a Transaction as Fraudulent, or Section 8.3, Identifying a Transaction as Legitimate.
You are required to add a manual alert if an unalerted transaction is reported as fraud.
Scenario ГўВЂВ“ Unalerted Transactions
Sometimes additional information becomes available on transactions that werenГўВЂВ™t alerted by the rules. A previously unalerted transaction could now be perceived as a scam, or a customer could report fraud on a previously unalerted transaction. In such instances, you must search for the unalerted transaction, create a manual alert, and identify it as fraud.
For previously unalerted transactions, youГўВЂВ™ll either see a set of two events for the same transaction in the Activity Timeline, paymentNRT, and paymentRT, or only one event for a mobile deposit transaction (checkDepositRT) or wire transaction (wireRT). These events display the transaction ID and amount:
checkDepositRT is the event for any mobile deposit that flows into EFM.
paymentRT is the event for the initiated Interac e-Transfer transaction that indicates the real time when the transaction was initiated.
paymentNRT is the event that indicates the payment went through to Interac.
wireRT is the event when a customer initiates a wire payment.
Important
:	You must only add manual alerts to checkDepositRT, paymentRT, or wireRT events. This ensures Counterparty or recipient details (names, email addresses, home addresses) are added to watchlists when you identify transactions as fraudulent. Do not add manual alerts to paymentNRT events.
To add a manual alert to the checkDepositRT, paymentRT, or wireRT event of an unalerted transaction:
Log in to EFM as described in Section 5.2, Logging in to Enterprise Fraud Management, then select
Incidents
on the Dashboard page.
Search for the transaction. See Section 8.11, Using the Search Function. Ensure you select the Routing/Customer ID (not the Routing/AccountID or Routing/CounterpartyAccountID).
ГўВ‘
Select the eye icon in the checkDepositRT, paymentRT, or wireRT transaction to open the sidebar. See FigureВ 25.
ГўВ‘ВЎ
Select Create alert.
ГўВ‘Вў
Comment
(strongly recommended) ГўВЂВ“ Enter a comment or select one from the template drop-down menu. Ensure comments are appropriate for EFM users and customers, clear, succinct, and descriptive. A comment cannot exceed 400 characters. Add an additional comment if needed.
Important
:	Comments are visible on other downstream systems such as the Interac eГўВЂВ‘Transfer Online Administration System (OAS) or other banking applications. Comments must be appropriate for both user and customer audiences.
ГўВ‘ВЈ
Select
Add alert
.
Figure 25: Adding a Manual Alert to an Unalerted Transaction
The Activity Timeline is updated with the manual alert. See Figure 26.
Figure 26: Manual Alert Shown in Activity Timeline
Identify the transaction as Risk in EFM. See Section 8.2, Identifying a Transaction as Fraudulent.
To add a manual alert to a fraudulent Auto Risk transaction:
Log in to EFM as described in Section 5.2, Logging in to Enterprise Fraud Management, then select
Incidents
on the Dashboard page.
Search for the transaction. See Section 8.11, Using the Search Function. Ensure you select the Routing/Customer ID (not the Routing/AccountID or Routing/CounterpartyAccountID).
ГўВ‘
Select the eye icon in the Auto Risk transaction to open the sidebar. See Figure 27.
ГўВ‘ВЎ
Select
Create alert
.
ГўВ‘Вў
Comment
(strongly recommended) ГўВЂВ“ Enter a comment or select one from the template drop-down menu. Ensure comments are appropriate for EFM users and customers, clear, succinct, and descriptive. A comment cannot exceed 400 characters. Add an additional comment if needed.
Important
: Comments are visible on other downstream systems such as the Interac eГўВЂВ‘Transfer Online Administration System (OAS) or other banking applications. Comments must be appropriate for both user and customer audiences.
ГўВ‘ВЈ
Select
Create alert
.
Figure 27: Adding a Manual Alert to an Auto Risk Transaction
The Auto Risk transaction now displays the manually added alert. See Figure 28.
Figure 28: Manual Alert added to Transaction Previously Flagged as Auto Risk
Identify the transaction as Risk in EFM. See Section 8.2, Identifying a Transaction as Fraudulent.
Related Information
Training Video for Adding a Manual Alert:
Fraud Alert Services in EFM
8.6	Adding Manual Alerts in Bulk
Users can add manually created alerts to non-alerted transactions in bulk if theyГўВЂВ™re initiated from the Incident Review page or from an Events search results page.
Important:
Only Junior Investigator and Senior Investigator user roles that have the Incident Bulk Manual Alert permission enabled can add manually created alerts in bulk.
Adding Manually Created Bulk Alerts from the Incident Page
To add manually created alerts in bulk from an Incident page:
Log in to EFM as described in Section 5.2, Logging in to Enterprise Fraud Management.
Select
Incidents
on the Dashboard page, or perform a search as described in Section 8.11, Using the Search Function. Select a
Customer ID
from
the Incident page or from your search results. See Figure 29.
Figure 29: Select a Customer ID
Create the manual alerts in bulk. See Figures 30 and 31.
ГўВ‘
Select the transactions from the Activity Timeline to apply a manually created alert to. See Figure 30.
ГўВ‘ВЎ
Select Actions, then select
+ Add alert(s) to # Event(s)
.
Figure 30: Selecting and Adding Alerts to Transactions in Bulk
Enter a comment or select one from the template drop-down menu, then select
Add Alerts
. See Figure 31. Ensure comments are appropriate for EFM users and customers, clear, succinct, and descriptive. A comment cannot exceed 400 characters. Add an additional comment if needed.
Important
:	Comments are visible on other downstream systems such as the Interac eГўВЂВ‘Transfer Online Administration System (OAS) or other banking applications. Comments must be appropriate for both user and customer audiences.
Figure 31: Providing a Comment and Adding Alerts
View the manually created alerts in the Activity Timeline. See Figure 32.
Figure 32: Manually Created Alerts Applied in Bulk
Identify the transactions as Risk in EFM. See Section 8.2, Identifying a Transaction as Fraudulent.
Adding Manually Created Bulk Alerts from the Events Search Results Page
To add manually created alerts in bulk from an Events search results page:
Log in to EFM as described in Section 5.2, Logging in to Enterprise Fraud Management.
Initiate a search. See Section 8.11, Using the Search Function. Select
Events
from your search results. See Figure 33.
Note:	Use search criteria that identifies the specific events/transactions by a common factor (e.g., transactions sent to a specific email address).
Figure 33: Events Search Results
Create the manual alerts in bulk. See Figures 34 and 35.
ГўВ‘
Select the events to apply manually created alerts to. See Figure 34.
ГўВ‘ВЎ
Select Actions, then select
Add Alerts to Selected Events
.
Figure 34: Selecting and Adding Alerts to Selected Events
Complete the Add Alerts pop-up window as follows. See Figure 35.
ГўВ€В’
Entity Type
ГўВЂВ“ Always select
Routing/Customer ID
as your Entity Type.
ГўВ€В’
Comment
(strongly recommended) ГўВЂВ“ Enter a comment or select one from the template drop-down menu. Ensure comments are appropriate for EFM users and customers, clear, succinct, and descriptive. A comment cannot exceed 400 characters. Add an additional comment if needed.
Important
:	Comments are visible on other downstream systems such as the Interac eГўВЂВ‘Transfer Online Administration System (OAS) or other banking applications. Comments must be appropriate for both user and customer audiences.
ГўВ€В’
Select
Add Alerts
.
Figure 35: Add Alerts Window
ГўВ€В’
View the manually created alerts for the selected transactions. See Figure 36.
Figure 36: Manually Created Alerts
Identify the transactions as Risk in EFM. See Section 8.2, Identifying a Transaction as Fraudulent, for more information.
8.7	Revising a Previously Identified Alert
If new information is received, you can revise any previously classified transaction in the incidentГўВЂВ™s Activity Timeline. For example, if an alert previously classified as legitimate is discovered to be fraudulent, you can change the classification to Risk. This only applies to incidents in the Closed list.
To change the identification of a transaction previously classified as No-risk:
Log in to EFM as described in Section 5.2, Logging in to Enterprise Fraud Management, then select
Incidents
on the Dashboard page.
Select the Customer ID in the Closed list to open the Incident review page. See Figure 37.
Figure 37: Selecting Customer ID
Locate the applicable transaction in the Activity Timeline. See Figure 38.
ГўВ‘

Edit
.

Risk
.

To request a recovery of funds for a confirmed Risk and completed Interac e-Transfer transaction that was created less than 90 days ago, select the Initiate Fund Recovery box. 8.8	Wires in EFM and the Wires Application
Only wires that are approved in the Wires application are visible in EFM. However, if a wire is alerted as potentially fraudulent in EFM, the status of the wire in both EFM and the Wires application may require investigation. There are various scenarios in which wires flow through both EFM and the Wires application that you should familiarize yourself with.
Scenario ГўВЂВ“ Expected Wire Flow
When a wire is initiated and approved in the Wires application, itГўВЂВ™s sent through EFM for a fraud check. After EFM validates the wire payment as legitimate, the transaction flows through to the next step in the process. See Figure 41.
Figure 41: Flow of Wires Experiencing No Friction
In EFM, the wire appears as an unalerted transaction. See Figure 42.
Figure 42: Unalerted Wire in EFM
If EFM deems the wire legitimate, and assuming there are no other blocks, the wire appears in the Submitted Wires list of the Wires application. It displays a Sent status. See Figure 43.
Figure 43: Unalerted Wire in the Wires Application
Scenario ГўВЂВ“ Alerted Wire
After a wire is initiated and approved through the Wires application, EFM performs its fraud check. If EFM identifies the transaction as suspicious, the wire is held until the wire is adjudicated and an alert is placed on the wire. An email notification is also sent to your financial institution indicating the wire is blocked, prompting you to adjudicate the transaction as either No-risk or Risk. See Figure 44.
Figure 44: Email Notification of Blocked Wire
After the email notification is received, identify the transaction as No-risk or Risk in EFM. If the wire is identified as No-risk, the wire continues its processing activities. If the wire is identified as Risk, the wire is declined and cancelled automatically. See Figure 45.
Figure 45: Flow of Alerted Wires
Before you identify the wire as fraudulent or legitimate, EFM displays the wire with an alert. See Figure 46.
Figure 46: Alerted Wire in EFM
At the same time, the alerted wire appears in the Submitted Wires list of the Wires application with a Pending status. See Figure 47.
Figure 47: Pending Wire in the Wires Application
Important
:	Wires that are alerted in EFM have a Pending status in the Wires application until you identify it as a legitimate or fraudulent transaction. After youГўВЂВ™ve performed your due diligence and followed your internal procedures, communicate your findings with the appropriate teams in your financial institution as needed. Some teams may require information from you to manage inquiries about the wire(s).
Scenario ГўВЂВ“ Alerted Wire Identified as No-risk
Following the notification that a wire has been blocked, perform your due diligence, and follow your internal procedures. Then identify the wire as No-risk using the side bar. See Figure 48.
Figure 48: Alerted Wire Identified as No-risk
EFM then displays the wire as No-risk. See Figure 49.
Figure 49: No-risk Identified Wire
After identifying the wire as No-risk, assuming there are no blocks (e.g., AML, etc.), the wireГўВЂВ™s status changes to Sent in the Wires application. This is seen in the Submitted Wires list of the Wires application. See Figure 50.
Figure 50: No-risk Wire in the Wires Application
Scenario ГўВЂВ“ Alerted Wire Identified as Risk
Following the notification that a wire has been blocked, perform your due diligence, and follow your internal procedures. Then identify the wire as Risk using the side bar. See Figure 51.
Figure 51: Alerted Wire Identified as Risk
EFM then displays the wire as Risk. See Figure 52.
Figure 52: Risk Identified Wire
After identifying the wire as Risk, the wire appears as Cancelled in the Submitted Wires list of the Wires application. See Figure 53.
Figure 53: Cancelled Wire in the Wires Application
Important
:	The Wires application isnГўВЂВ™t connected to your banking system. Follow your internal procedures for managing cancelled wires including settlement to the customerГўВЂВ™s account.
Scenario ГўВЂВ“ Subsequently Alerted Wire
When a wire is initiated and approved in the Wires application, itГўВЂВ™s sent to EFM for a fraud check. If your customer previously submitted a wire that was alerted in EFM and it hasnГўВЂВ™t been adjudicated yet, any subsequently submitted wires are deemed suspicious and are alerted. The wire is held until itГўВЂВ™s adjudicated. See Figure 54.
Figure 54: Subsequently Alerted Wire Flow
EFM displays the wires as alerted transactions. See Figure 55.
Figure 55: Subsequently Alerted Wire in EFM
Any alerted wire is held until the wire is adjudicated as No-risk or Risk. If itГўВЂВ™s deemed No-risk, the wire is released and continues through settlement processing. If itГўВЂВ™s deemed Risk, the wire is cancelled. Follow your internal procedures for managing cancelled wires including settlement to the customerГўВЂВ™s account. Additionally, the alerted wire appears in the Submitted Wires list of the Wires application with a Pending status. Depending on how the wire is adjudicated, the status changes to Sent or Cancelled. See Figure 56.
Figure 56: Pending Wire in the Wires Application
At the same time, an email notification is sent to your fraud team with the wire details and notice that the wire was blocked. This prompts you to adjudicate the alerted wire as No-risk or Risk. See Figure 57.
Figure 57: Rejected Email Notification
Important
:	Subsequently sent wires in EFM have a Pending status in the Wires application until you identify the alerted wires as a legitimate or fraudulent transaction.
Important
:	The Wires application isnГўВЂВ™t connected to your banking system. Follow your internal procedures for managing cancelled wires including settlement to the customerГўВЂВ™s account.
Scenario ГўВЂВ“ Fraudulently Created Wire
If a wire was fraudulently created, adding a manual alert to the wire in EFM is required. See FigureВ 58.
Figure 58: Manual Alert Applied to Wire
Ensure the manual alert is applied to the wires payment event (wireRT) and identify it as Risk. See Figure 59. See Section 8.5, Adding Manual Alerts to Unalerted Transactions, for more information.
Figure 59: Identifying the wireRT Event as Risk
In the Submitted Wires list of the Wires application, the wire has a Sent status. See Figure 60.
Figure 60: Identifying the wireRT Event as Risk
Important
: Do not use the Initiate Fund Recovery box for wire transactions. See Section 8.12, Recovery of Funds for more information.
Funds can only be recovered on a best-efforts basis. See Section 8.12, Recovery of Funds, for more information.
Important
:	When a manual alert is applied to a wire, adjudicating the alert as No-risk or Risk doesnГўВЂВ™t stop the flow of the wire. If a recovery of funds is needed for a fraudulent wire, follow your existing recovery of funds process.
8.9	Mobile Deposits in EFM
Mobile Deposits are cheques, money orders, drafts, or Receiver General Warrants that customers deposit to their accounts at your financial institution using Deposit Anywhere on a mobile device. All Canadian dollar deposits processed through Deposit Anywhere are visible in EFM. EFM captures mobile deposits in monitor mode. In Monitor Mode, EFM generates alerts for any suspicious mobile deposit transactions and an email notification is sent to your fraud analyst(s), advising them to review the alerted transaction(s). See Figure 61.
Important
:	Deposits made through Branch Capture (BRDC) or Corporate Capture (CRDC) arenГўВЂВ™t monitored through EFM.
Important
:	EFM doesnГўВЂВ™t interrupt Deposit AnywhereГўВЂВ™s deposit process and only captures mobile deposits in monitor mode.
Figure 61: Example of Email Notification for Alerted Cheque Deposits
In EFM, the ГўВЂВњcheckDepositRTГўВЂВќ event type is visible for any mobile deposit that flows into EFM. See Figure 62.
Figure 62: The CheckDepositRT Event Type
EFM creates an alert for suspicious mobile deposits. See Figure 63. You must identify alerted transactions as Risk or No-risk. This process is similar to the current process used for Interac e-Transfer transactions. See Section 8.2, Identifying a Transaction as Fraudulent, for more information.
Figure 63: Alerted Mobile Deposit Transaction
Important
:	Do not use the Initiate Fund Recovery box for mobile deposit transactions. See Section 8.12, Recovery of Funds, for more information.
If an alerted mobile deposit isnГўВЂВ™t marked as Risk or No-risk before a subsequent transaction occurs on EFM, any subsequent transactions are automatically flagged with an ГўВЂВњAlertГўВЂВќ. You must identify the initial alerted transaction and any other alerted transactions that occur after it. See Figure 64.
Figure 64: Initial and Subsequent Alerted Mobile Transactions
For fraudulent, non-alerted mobile deposit transactions, you must create a manual alert for the transactions, identify the mobile deposits as Risk, and add any relevant information to them. See Figure 65. This process is similar to the current process used for Interac e-Transfer transactions. See Section 8.2, Identifying a Transaction as Fraudulent, or Section 8.5, Adding Manual Alerts to Unalerted Transactions, for more information.
Figure 65: Identifying a Mobile Deposit as Risk
8.10	Bill Payments in EFM
Bill payments, regardless of which submission method was used to submit it to Central 1, are visible in EFM. EFM generates alerts for any suspected fraudulent bill payments and all alerted bill payments must be investigated.
In EFM, the ГўВЂВњBillPayRTГўВЂВќ event type is visible for any bill payment that flows into EFM. See FigureВ 66.
Figure 66: Example of BillPayRT Event Type
When EFM generates an alert for a bill payment, an email notification is sent to your fraud analyst(s) advising them to review the alerted bill payment. See Figure 67.
Figure 67: Bill Payment Notification Email
Alerted bill payments must be investigated and identified as Risk or No-risk, preferably in a timely manner. You can identify or re-identify a bill payment as many times as you see fit as Risk or No-Risk up until just before the billerГўВЂВ™s specific cut-off time. When identifying any alerted bill payment as Risk, you must add any relevant information to it. If your investigation deems a non-alerted bill payment as fraudulent, you must create a manual alert for the transaction, identify the bill payment as Risk, and add any relevant information to it. See Figure 68. This process is similar to the current process used for Interac e-Transfer transactions. See Section 8.2, Identifying a Transaction as Fraudulent, or Section 8.5, Adding Manual Alerts to Unalerted Transactions, for more information.
Figure 68: Identifying a Bill Payment as Risk
Important
:
Most
alerted bill payments must be identified before 11:59 am PT
. If an alerted bill payment is not identified before this time, the bill payment is released and continues its processing. However,
select billers have their own specific cut off times and must be identified before those specified cut off times
. See Section 1.4, Bill Payment Cut-Off Times, of the Electronic Bill Payments System Part for information.
Important: If you use real time bill payment processing, any alerted bill payment can be subject to Auto Risk alerts
. See Figure 69 and Section 8.4, Identifying Alerts in Auto Risk Transactions, for information. Currently the Auto Risk alerts feature isnГўВЂВ™t applicable to batch bill payment processing. Central 1 will notify EFM users when the Auto Risk alerts feature applies to batch bill payment processing
.
Figure 69: Auto Risk Bill Payment
Related Information
Bill Payment Cut-Off Times
8.11	Using the Search Function
Currently, EFM captures Interac e-Transfer payments initiated by the sender in online banking and any inbound or outbound wire payments. The following transactions wonГўВЂВ™t be found in EFM:
Unalerted transactions older than 90 days
Interac e-Transfer payments where the account and the financial institution are the receiving party
To search for incidents within 90 days, you can use the Account ID, Transaction ID, or Entity ID. For incidents older than 90 days, search on the Entity ID (this will only produce results if there are alerted transactions in the incident).
The search function in EFM allows you to perform the following:
Perform a single search by name using accountName or counterpartyName
Note:	When creating a search by name using ГўВЂВњaccountNameГўВЂВќ or ГўВЂВњcounterpartyNameГўВЂВќ, the specific account name or counter party name must include quotation marks around it, otherwise the search will be unsuccessful. See Figure 70.
Figure 70: Searching by accountName or counterpartyName
Search by the Entity ID
Search by the Transaction ID number from the Online Administrative System (OAS)
To search for a transaction in EFM:
Log in to EFM as described in Section 5.2, Logging in to Enterprise Fraud Management, then select
Incidents
on the Dashboard page.
Select the search box. See Figure 71. This opens the selection of search criteria.
Figure 71: Select Search Box
Note:	When the search box is populated (
ГўВ‘
), the navigation buttons at the top of the page convert to a hamburger menu (
ГўВ‘ВЎ
). See FigureВ 72.
If you want to navigate to a different page, while you have criteria in the search box:
ГўВ‘Вў
Select the Menu to display the navigation buttons.
ГўВ‘ВЈ
Delete criteria from the Search box to return to the default position.
Figure 72: Menu Toggle Option
To locate a customerГўВЂВ™s account and specific transaction:
ГўВ‘
Enter the search criteria (e.g., Account ID or OAS Transaction ID). See Figure 73.
Note:	FAS alert notifications use either the Interac ID reference number (e.g., CAXXXXXXXXXX) or the wire transaction ID number (e.g., C1XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX). EFM alert notifications use the transaction ID (e.g., 20230221XXXXXX) or the wire transaction ID number.
ГўВ‘ВЎ
Select the Routing/CustomerID
in the Search Preview section.
Figure 73: Searching for Customer Account ID
If you donГўВЂВ™t see the transaction, use the Activity filter to ensure Non-alerted is selected under Activity, then locate the applicable transaction. See Figure 74.
Note:	Any updates you make to a transaction must be on the checkRT, paymentRT or wireRT event.
Figure 74: Routing/Customer ID ГўВЂВ“ Use Activity Filter
8.12	Recovery of Funds
At times, you may need to manually request a recovery of funds.
Interac e-Transfer Transactions
Only fraudulent, completed Interac e-Transfer transactions are eligible for a recovery of funds. If a recovery of funds is needed for a confirmed Risk and completed Interac e-Transfer transaction that was created fewer than 90 days ago, select the Initiate Fund Recovery box during the adjudication process. See Figure 75.
Important
:	The Initiate Fund Recovery box is strictly intended for confirmed Risk and completed Interac e-Transfer transactions created fewer than 90 days ago. Typically, confirmed Risk and completed Interac e-Transfer transactions have had manual alerts added to them.
Important
:	EFM users with OAS access who are assigned Fraud Analyst roles must initiate funds recovery requests through OAS instead of using EFMГўВЂВ™s Initiate Fund Recovery box. See the Interac eГўВЂВ‘Transfer Online Administration System Part for more information.
Figure 75: Initiate Fund Recovery Box
After identifying an Interac e-Transfer transaction as Risk, an automated email with the transactionГўВЂВ™s details is sent to Central 1ГўВЂВ™s FAS team. EFM also sends existing email contacts a copy of the automated email. See Figure 76. The FAS Team then begins the recovery of funds process.
Figure 76: Example of Automated Initiate Fund Recovery Email Request
If a recovery of funds is needed for an Interac e-Transfer transaction that was created more than 90 days ago and it canГўВЂВ™t be located in EFM, email the FAS Team at
FAS@central1.com
. Include the Transaction ID, the senderГўВЂВ™s name, the amount of the transaction, and a note that states the request is for a recovery of funds.
Note:	A completed transaction is when a paymentRT (without an alert) and a paymentNRT event occur and the recipient receives the funds. The completion of the transaction can also be verified by looking at the transactionГўВЂВ™s status on OAS.
Central 1ГўВЂВ™s FAS Team manages recovery of funds requests. Once a request is received, the FAS Team adds a message to the Incident Activity Timeline stating, ГўВЂВњFund recovery request submittedГўВЂВќ. EFM users who receive alerts will also receive a copy of this email. The FAS Team will receive an update from the receiving financial institution regarding the availability of funds for recovery and any details of the indemnity letter. Afterwards, the FAS Team
adds a comment to the EFM alerts with ГўВЂВњAmount Available + Contacts for Indemnity LetterГўВЂВќ and
emails your financial institution using EFM with the details.
You may find that a transaction was completed, but EFM didnГўВЂВ™t create an alert for it when it was needed. This is known as a fraudulent, unalerted transaction. If the transaction was created fewer than 90 days ago, create a manual alert on the transaction, identify the transaction as Risk, and select the Initiate Fund Recovery box.
To request a recovery of funds for a fraudulent, unalerted transaction that was created more than 90 days since it was created, email
FAS@central1.com
ГўВЃ
with the Transaction ID, the senderГўВЂВ™s name, the amount of the transaction, and that this is a recovery of funds request. The FAS Team then requests a recovery of funds and the receiving institution is notified by Interac
of this request. Any attempt to recover funds is done on a best efforts basis. If the funds are available for recovery, the FAS Team confirms this with you.
If the transaction is confirmed cancelled, you may need to manually process the posting from your general ledger to the customerГўВЂВ™s account. Refer to OAS or your daily settlement reports if needed.
Bill Payment Transactions
You may find that a bill payment requires a recovery of funds. Follow your internal policies and procedures regarding recovery of funds options for confirmed and/or suspected fraudulent bill payments.
Mobile Deposit Transactions
You may find that a mobile deposit requires a recovery of funds. Follow your internal policies and procedures regarding recovery of funds options for confirmed and/or suspected fraudulent mobile deposits.
Wire Transactions
You may find that a wire transaction requires a recovery of funds. Funds can only be recovered on a best-efforts basis by initiating a Recall Request. See the Wires Part for more information.
Related Information
Wires Part
